PixiJS V5 Tutorial (22) - Debugging Pixi, and Meet dat.GUI
Next up, let's get familiar with how to debug things in Pixi.

Honestly, debugging while developing with Pixi is a real pain. Pixi's structure isn't like HTML and CSS — you can't pop open devTools and see a DOM tree or tweak the related parameters there. So when you want to adjust something during development, it's actually pretty rough.
This post is mostly about getting to know dat.GUI. We'll save the actual implementation for the next one.
If you search around for Pixi dev tools, most people end up recommending
. A lot of folks who've been doing this a while suggest using it as your Pixi debugging tool.
Day18 超好用工具dat.GUI (article in Mandarin)
[PixiJS - Day-16] 使用 dat.GUI 工具除錯 (article in Mandarin)
The cool thing is dat.GUI isn't limited to Pixi — you can use it with Three.js too, and the wildest part is you can even use it when building VR.
If dat.GUI for VR sounds interesting, you can
and dig into the code — it's open source.

The biggest selling point of dat.GUI is that you get a graphical interface to adjust your parameters and objects. Without it, normal development means you edit, then save, then refresh the screen — and after enough rounds of that it gets tedious and your productivity tanks.
you can see some examples. You tweak the floating panel on the left and it changes the graphics on screen — things like Message and speed.
With this GUI panel, adjusting PixiJS gets a lot easier.
That's enough of a basic intro to dat.GUI for now. In the next post, let's look at how to actually wire it in.

Debugging in Pixi is genuinely painful — there's no DOM to inspect in devTools, so tweaking parameters is a slog of edit, save, refresh, repeat. This post introduces dat.GUI, a tool that gives you a graphical panel to adjust your objects live. We'll get familiar with it here and actually wire it into Pixi in the next post.
